chapters.title: Yoga shapes both body and mood
chapters.text:
When people think about yoga, they often assume it only stretches the muscles and the back. Yoga goes much further than that and reaches the whole body, including the brain. People who keep up a yoga practice tend to have healthy levels of serotonin, which is the hormone tied to mood. The same practice helps lower cortisol, the stress hormone. On the whole, yoga supports the health of our nerve cells, so we work better and feel better.

The brain keeps forming new links through life, and those links help us stay sharp. Studies have shown that yoga supports the hippocampus, the part of the brain in charge of building new links between nerve cells, which is why memory often improves with practice.

Yoga also has an effect on metabolism. Because the practice and the meditation calm the body, the thyroid, which runs metabolism, slows down just a little. That lets the body pull more from the food it takes in. Over time, this can help with weight.

After a regular yoga practice, both muscles and brain grow stronger.

Breathing plays a key part in yoga as well. Yogis teach that by practicing pranayama, which means shaping the breath, a person becomes more relaxed and more focused. They pay close attention to the long exhale, since the heart is calmer when we breathe out than when we breathe in.

This summary will help you see how yoga shapes the body and the mind. You are holding the key to less stress and better health.

A small fact for the road: the human body has 206 bones.

chapters.title: Yoga lifts immunity and sharpens the brain
chapters.text:
What if someone told you that yoga could even shape your immune response? That is not the same as saying you will never need a doctor or a pill, since yoga is no cure-all. What it can do is lower inflammation in the body. When we get sick, the body sends out cytokines, which are proteins that tell the immune system to react. Too many cytokines can lead to long-term inflammation, so a yoga mat at home is a good way to keep that in check.

As noted earlier, yoga is not only about the body. Along with pranayama, try belly breathing, and do not be fooled by the name, since you do not actually breathe into your stomach. With this method, you let the belly move out as you breathe in, which gives the diaphragm, the muscle under the lungs, more room to expand the chest.

Getting more oxygen into the blood through good breathing helps the heart work better. Yoga is known to raise heart rate variability and to lower blood pressure. Many people live with high blood pressure, and yoga gives them a way to care for the heart without much cost. Still, it is smart to talk to a doctor before you take on a new physical practice.

Yoga can lift the health of your heart and your blood pressure.

Yoga can also shape the urinary system. Some conditions throw that system off balance, and yoga can help restore tone to the muscles. Some researchers note that yoga may also reach the muscles of a woman's pelvis, which can support fertility.

A quick note: most of us take roughly 15 to 20 breaths a minute.

chapters.title: Sitting poses and how they work
chapters.text:
Now let us look at some of the most common sitting poses, called asanas. One of the easiest is Cat, where you kneel with your hands and knees on the floor and round your back. The shape helps you breathe out more fully and tones the muscles in your belly. The pose most often paired with Cat is Cow. The key difference is in the spine, since Cow asks you to arch your back and open your chest, which lets the breath move in more deeply.

Yoga builds the bridge between mind and body by teaching you to listen, as Ann Swanson has said.

Yoga is slow, careful training of the muscles, done at a steady pace with full attention to which parts of the body work and which rest.

Side Bend is a friend to anyone who spends the day at a desk. It stretches the back and helps the muscles reach positions they rarely visit in daily life. We sit for hours, tightening the back muscles and never letting them relax, which leads to cramps and soreness. Side Bend lengthens the back by stretching the arm over the head toward the leg on the same side. The move supports the health of the spine and the disks between the bones.

During yoga, you have to be careful. Slow movement is the rule, since rushing only invites injury.

One of the most calming poses is Child's Pose, which settles the mind and loosens the back after a long day. You kneel and stretch the arms forward, and the body uses the smallest number of muscles, so the neck and back can rest.

chapters.title: Standing poses and their effects
chapters.text:
For standing poses, you have probably tried Forward Fold at least once. The move asks you to wrap your arms around your knees, hugging the legs like a long-lost friend, and press your head toward them, which builds flexibility and stretches the back, the legs, and the seat. If your back aches, it is better to rest the hands on the knees and keep the spine long.

The next move is called Chair, which fires up the body and tones the thighs. Line up the knees with the pelvis and lift the arms straight up. The pose raises blood pressure and strengthens the hips, but if you already have high blood pressure, keep the hands on the hips and skip the arm lift. Chair can also take pressure off sore knees.

Warrior II helps you steady the body. Bend one knee, straighten the other leg, and hold the arms parallel to the floor. Aim to send your weight down through the feet. The pose sharpens the mind and brings a lift of energy.

Tree builds balance, calms the mind, and stretches the hips, which adds flexibility. Rest one foot on the inside of the opposite knee and bring the hands together in front of the chest as if in prayer. The body will wobble, since you are asking the muscles of one leg to hold the whole body. Keep the breath moving and never hold it, the way many people do when they feel unsteady.

Steady breathing during the poses lifts the whole body, since oxygen reaches the brain faster.

chapters.title: Yoga is good for the back and the neck
chapters.text:
Swanson suggests paying close attention to the body during the practice and learning where your flexibility and strength end, so you can avoid injury. Build the muscles step by step and never rush.

Yoga has been shown over and over to grow flexibility, so a tight body only gives you more reason to start, as Ann Swanson has said.

If you think you are not flexible enough for yoga, relax. Yoga is designed to grow your flexibility and your strength, and you will bend a little more with every week of practice. If you have a health issue that needs a doctor's input, ask a professional about how yoga might shape your body. A doctor can help you pick the poses that help and the ones to skip.

It is important to know when a stretch has gone too far. If you feel sharp pain near the joints, ease off. A good stretch tightens the muscles and aches a little, while an overstretch tears fibers and leaves the joints exposed.

Do not push the body past its edge, and remember that in yoga, the slower you go, the better.

Many people feel neck pain from the bent posture that comes with hours of phone use. Yoga can help with that. The poses strengthen the muscles of the neck and the back and give you more control over your posture. You will start to notice when you lean toward the phone, and you will straighten the neck on your own.

Back pain is another common problem. Yoga eases the pain and tones the muscles of the back, which leaves people with more energy and focus. Swanson points out that yoga helps children too, since long school days mean too little movement and weak muscles. Yoga lifts their health and also raises their self-awareness, since the practice teaches them to pay attention.

chapters.title: Mindfulness is the way to handle stress
chapters.text:
Yoga slows down the loss of bone and muscle that comes with age, which is why it helps prevent early aging and supports the brain. With a steady practice, you can keep your bones and muscles stronger and stay more active in later life.

Yoga also prepares the body for meditation, and a body at rest makes the mind easier to settle. The most common form of meditation in this practice is mindfulness, where you let go of judgment about what is happening and simply watch the moment. Mindfulness lifts focus and memory and lowers tiredness and worry.

Many people think they cannot meditate because their minds are too busy. The author makes clear that every mind is full of thoughts all the time, and you cannot turn that off, since that is not how the brain works. Meditation is about watching the thoughts come and go. It teaches us how to handle the hard parts of life. People often react sharply to a problem, and that reaction wears the body down. That is why it matters to practice mindfulness and build a calmer response to stress.

Try to look at your problems without judgment and see what the hard moments can teach you.

Yoga makes you more stress-resistant through both body and mind. The body needs activity, and the poses give it that. You will support the work of the muscles and organs with regular practice. A relaxed body, though, does not always mean a relaxed mind, so the mind needs care too. People often hold tension in the muscles through the day because of all the stress they live with, and yoga helps let that tension go. You may notice that the link between body and mind runs deep, and that is why yoga and movement lift the mood when life feels heavy. The moment you let stress hormones out through movement, the heart feels lighter too.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
The biggest gift of yoga is the better health of both body and mind. Yoga fits almost anyone, including children. The poses help people strengthen different parts of the body, and they can pick the ones that target the muscles they want to work. Because each move is done slowly and with care, yoga helps avoid injury and the heavy soreness that comes from overdoing it.

Practicing different poses can lower your stress. You can buy a mat, find a clear video online led by a trained teacher, and unwind after a long day. You can pick sitting poses when you feel too tired for standing ones. As you move, pay attention to what the muscles are doing. Which muscles ache and stretch, and which muscles stay quiet during a pose? That kind of awareness teaches you how the body works. Staying focused on the present lifts the mood too. You send the attention to the body and let the troubles fade, even if only for an hour.

There is another reason yoga lifts the mind. Yogis often practice mindfulness, which is being fully in the moment without judgment. With regular meditation, people build a stronger response to stress, and they are no longer held hostage by the noise in their heads. Yoga helps people grow kinder toward themselves, and they become less harsh and less tense about their problems.

On top of the body and mind benefits, yoga helps you stay focused and productive. If your attention keeps getting pulled away by distractions, including your phone, try a few minutes of movement. You will be surprised by how much your focus and follow-through can grow. Yoga reaches into many corners of life, from relationships to health to work to rest to the mood, and you can even use it as a quiet form of therapy for the small daily troubles.

A small practice to try. Pick the most comfortable pose and settle into it while you keep the breath steady. Try belly breathing at a slow pace and fully empty the lungs, so carbon dioxide does not build up in the blood. Stay aware of the body as you move, and do everything with care. After the practice, you should feel more relaxed and more present.

If you read to the end, well done.
